Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty hands-on and scattered. I'd dedicate around 32 hours a week, but it wasn't always at a desk with the LMS open. I learn best by doing things physically—writing notes by hand, building models, practicing problems on paper. I'd often download what I needed and then work through it away from my computer, maybe at a café or even while commuting. The routine existed, but it wasn't always visible online.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was happening in my life. At 26, I've got responsibilities beyond just coursework—work commitments, family stuff. When deadlines loomed, I'd cram more intensely, but otherwise I tried to spread things out. Some weeks I was consistent, other weeks life just took over and I had to adapt on the fly.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or needing to grab a specific resource that got me to log in. I wasn't someone who checked the LMS daily just to browse. If I needed lecture slides or had to submit something, that's when I'd pop in. Otherwise, I was working from downloaded materials or my own notes.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a habit for me. I'd use it when necessary, but I preferred working with physical materials. The few times I logged in more regularly were when discussion posts were required, but even then it felt more obligatory than natural.
Q5: The video lectures were genuinely useful—I watched about 83 minutes worth because I could pause, rewind, and take handwritten notes at my own pace. The discussion forums were okay too; I posted 14 times because hearing different perspectives helped solidify concepts. Anything I could interact with actively felt more valuable to me as a kinesthetic learner.
Q6: I largely ignored the reading materials posted as PDFs that I couldn't annotate easily online. I'd download them once and never look at them in the LMS again. Some of the supplementary links felt like busywork, so I'd skip those unless they seemed directly relevant to exams. If it didn't feel hands-on or practical, I probably didn't engage with it much.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to passing the exam or understanding something I'd struggled with. If it involved doing something—solving problems, building something, applying a concept—I was in. Passive reading or watching without purpose just didn't click for me.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. With high stress and competing demands, I had to prioritize what counted toward my grade. Required activities got done because they had to, but optional ones often fell by the wayside unless they genuinely interested me or seemed like they'd help with exams.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides, print them out, and annotate by hand. I used physical textbooks and even created flashcards. I'd practice problems on paper, work through concepts by drawing diagrams. The LMS was just a starting point for gathering materials.
Q10: There were entire study sessions where I'd be working through problems at my desk for hours, but the LMS would show nothing because I wasn't logged in. I'd review printed notes on the bus, discuss concepts with friends over coffee, or watch downloaded videos offline. My engagement was real, just not tracked.
Q11: Yeah, constantly. I'd access a video or PDF once, download it, and then study from that local copy for days or weeks. Why keep logging in when I already have what I need? It's more efficient for me to work offline where I can focus without the distraction of being online.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially when stress peaked. My attendance dropped to about 49% because sometimes I just couldn't make it—whether from burnout, work conflicts, or feeling overwhelmed. When things piled up, logging into the LMS felt like one more thing on an endless list, so I'd retreat to simpler offline studying.
Q13: Definitely. High stress made me avoid the LMS because it represented all the things I was behind on. When I was fatigued or confused, I'd rather work through my own notes than face the overwhelming list of incomplete tasks online. Competing responsibilities meant I had to triage, and the LMS often lost that battle.
Q14: There were times I'd scroll through discussion posts or skim a video without really absorbing anything. My mind would be elsewhere—stressed about other things. I'd technically be 'active' but not actually learning. It was just going through the motions to feel like I was doing something.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments even when I knew they mattered. My assignment completion was only around 15%, which I'm not proud of. Sometimes the anxiety of starting something overwhelming made avoidance feel easier in the moment. I'd tell myself I'd do it later, and later never came. My resilience usually kicked in eventually, but not always in time.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the main driver of my LMS activity. I'd log in more frequently as due dates approached, cramming to submit what I could. Without a deadline breathing down my neck, I had little motivation to engage with the platform. It wasn't healthy, but it's how I operated.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect. When I got good feedback, it motivated me briefly. But honestly, with my low motivation overall, even positive feedback didn't sustain engagement for long. Sometimes I didn't check feedback at all because I was already onto the next crisis.
Q18: The course structure felt a bit rigid for my learning style. I needed more hands-on, practical components rather than reading-heavy modules. The 20 online courses I was managing meant I couldn't give each one the attention it deserved. A more flexible structure with practical applications would've helped me stay engaged.
Q19: The video content was helpful because I could control the pace. But the overall design assumed consistent online engagement, which didn't match how I learn. More downloadable, interactive materials would've been better. The discussion requirements were okay, but sometimes felt forced rather than organic.
Q20: I think that happens when students are anxious or stressed and keep checking the LMS without actually being able to focus on completing work. They might be looking for clarity, procrastinating productively, or just feeling overwhelmed. High activity doesn't mean productive activity—sometimes it's just spinning wheels.
Q21: That's pretty much my situation. My exam score was decent at nearly 62%, and I passed, but my LMS engagement was low. I studied a lot offline—the hours were there, just not visible. Some of us learn better outside the system but can still pull through when it counts.
Q22: Course logs can show login patterns and submission times, which might indicate deadline-driven behavior. But they miss the depth of offline engagement, the quality of learning, and the personal circumstances affecting a student. They see clicks, not comprehension. They track presence, not understanding.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm actively wrestling with concepts, asking questions, connecting ideas to real applications. For me, it's hands-on involvement—writing, practicing, discussing. It's not about how often I log in; it's about whether the learning sticks.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show the hours I spent with printed materials at my kitchen table, the study groups with friends, the mindfulness exercises I did to manage stress, or the genuine happiness I felt when concepts finally clicked. It wouldn't capture my resilience in pushing through despite high stress, or the fact that I was balancing this with a full life at 26. My engagement was real—it just lived mostly offline.
